ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for pappardelle ragù bianco. FOODON_00002572 (pasta food product) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Pasta, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for pappardelle with white meat ragù and pecorino.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 229961009 (Pasta dish) is used as the closest parent. No specific concept exists for pappardelle ragù bianco.

serving.natural_language_portion: One restaurant serving of pappardelle ragù bianco with pecorino is approximately 350 g, comprising a generous portion of wide egg pasta ribbons topped with braised pork ragù and grated aged Pecorino cheese.

nutrient_source.title: Pasta with meat sauce, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with Pecorino Romano cheese (USDA FDC #173420) and pork loin braised (USDA FDC #174032) component estimates
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry exists for pappardelle ragù bianco with pecorino. Values are estimated by weighted component analysis: homemade egg pappardelle (~45% by weight, cooked), braised Mangalica pork loin and Italian sausage (~30%), Pecorino Romano cheese (~8%), olive oil and aromatics (~17%). USDA SR Legacy entries used: pasta cooked (FDC #20121), pork loin braised (FDC #174032), Italian pork sausage cooked (FDC #174337), Pecorino Romano (FDC #173420), olive oil (FDC #04053). Energy cross-checked via Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — pasta (egg-based, cooked al dente) GI approximately 40–50
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pappardelle ragù bianco with pecorino. GI of 45 is estimated from egg-based pasta cooked al dente (GI range 40–50, mean ~45) per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The protein and fat content of the ragù and cheese further attenuate postprandial glucose response. GL per serving calculated as: (45 × 20.5 g carbohydrate per 100g × 350 g serving) / 100 = 32.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from cured sausage, aged Pecorino Romano, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A 350 g serving provides approximately 1330 mg sodium, which represents 58% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and avoid adding extra salt or high-sodium condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from egg pasta with low GI attenuation by protein and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 45 and a glycemic load of ~32.5 g per restaurant serving, this dish poses a moderate postprandial glucose challenge; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a reduced portion of approximately 150–200 g and pair with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from pork sausage, Pecorino Romano, and cholesterol content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A full restaurant serving contributes approximately 10.9 g saturated fat and 217 mg cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption and choose smaller portions to stay within AHA saturated fat guidelines of less than 13 g per day.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pappardelle Ragù Bianco with Pecorino is a traditional Tuscan Italian pasta dish featuring wide, flat egg pasta ribbons (pappardelle) served with a white meat ragù — a slow-braised sauce made without tomato, using white wine, Mangalica pork loin, Italian pork sausage, and aromatic herbs such as rosemary, sage, and thyme. The dish is finished with generous shavings or grating of aged Pecorino Romano cheese. Unlike the more internationally familiar tomato-based ragù, ragù bianco (white ragù) relies on the natural sweetness of braised pork, the acidity of white wine, and the umami depth of aged sheep's milk cheese for its flavour profile. The texture is rich and silky, with the wide pasta ribbons providing a substantial chew that holds the meat sauce well. Nutritionally, a restaurant serving (~350 g) provides approximately 693 kcal, 37.8 g protein, 28.7 g fat (10.9 g saturated), and 71.8 g carbohydrate. The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low), reflecting the egg pasta base cooked al dente and the moderating effect of protein and fat from the meat and cheese. Sodium content is elevated (~1330 mg per serving) due to the cured sausage and aged Pecorino, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with gluten, egg, or dairy allergies. It is a culturally significant preparation from Tuscany, where white ragù is a hallmark of the regional cucina povera tradition.

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry exists in any approved primary source for pappardelle ragù bianco with Mangalica pork and Pecorino as served at Ricciotti Singapore. Values are estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked egg pasta, braised pork loin, Italian pork sausage, and Pecorino Romano. GI is analogously estimated from egg-based pasta GI data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not consistently reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries used for this estimation. Therefore, it is considered not reported for this composite dish. All other -1 values have been replaced with estimated numeric values from component analysis. Nutrient values represent a weighted composite estimate for the dish as described; actual restaurant preparation may vary. Serving size of 350 g is estimated based on typical Italian restaurant pasta portion norms; the field survey listing does not specify a gram weight. GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per 100g × serving_g) / 100 = (45 × 20.5 × 350) / 100 = 32.5 g. The cuisine_tag 'Chicken Dish' in the input record appears to be a platform tagging error and has been disregarded. This dish contains pork and is not halal.
